title: Building of www.WorldLit.com
excerpt: I never thought I would be calling it worldlit.com, feels like the world is on fire :)
body:
I never thought I would be calling it worldlit.com, feels like the world is on fire :) yeah! it will be.

Until now I have been writing the code using Deepseek and it's pretty successful. Ah! it's more that I intended. I felt broken, how such a thing could be even possible so I wrote this-

I wrote but it wrote it all,
I thought but it found it all,
I learned but it knew it all,
So I used it, all in all.
What original could I be, it wrote it all in mere glee
More than all that I could think, feel or write
I feel like a trance in a cave and it is all that is bright
I am done. Done for the day
Wait! There is, There is still a reason to be gay
It is a lot more than I am now
It is even more than what I was
But it is nothing compared to what I could be.
An inferno from hell or an eternal flower of glee.

Should I use some theory to structure my arguments then what good I hold in the face of this AI. We , the modern being are so afraid of AI just because we had turned ourselves into machines and AI promises to do the same. No! I am not a machine, I am much more than that.

June 24, 2026

Well I am back from my NET prep. I'll be using this website as my reference material. I should be fast, concise and emotionally connected. It doesn't need to hold all the literature in the world but what I need.

I read Ulysses when a great literature was in my need but for sure it was not a great deed. I read it again and again but for me there was no joy to gain. It was alien and far too removed from awareness of mine what better a dollar is for me than a dime. I don't know what Dublin is or was, what joy I can find in mundane if not in the greatest of cause. I know about Delhi or Lucknow or London at far but Dublin! it's no more than a fancy imported car. I dropped it just to look for the context, you can kill the author but can't detach the reader from the text.

No you can't read a poem without the context, how could you explore just a symphony of few words, you got to have the connections in your mind that trigger the same response as intended. No you can not read a poetry out of dark, you have to know about the skylark.

It is the novels and the stories of universal that grapples the mind of the ordinary. Oh my life what better could I find than mere existence. What is better that to breathe, to be free from pain, to be able to see, to be able to think. I want to indulge in the ordinary universal. Not the courts but the homes where I have lived. No! the literature is not in strange but in familiar. Strange is all that is beyond understanding of mine. I want to delve in the universal. The very pains and joys of life, the very plains and hills in sight, the very sweets and bitters of taste, the very calms and moments of haste. I want the life on the paper.

It is not what I like, it is what I want..... mind it!

title: Net Cracker
excerpt: For sure this exam is vast if I take the wholeness of English. Here is the list of the authors and their works.
body:
For sure this exam is vast if I take the wholeness of English. Here is the list of the authors and their works.

I. Old English & Medieval (c. 650–1500)

Beowulf Poet – Beowulf (anonymous)
Caedmon – Old English religious poems
Cynewulf – Old English religious poems
Geoffrey Chaucer – The Canterbury Tales, Troilus and Criseyde
William Langland – Piers Plowman
John Gower – Confessio Amantis
Pearl Poet – Sir Gawain and the Green Knight
Thomas Malory – Le Morte d'Arthur
John Wycliffe – Bible translator, reformer

II. Renaissance & Elizabethan (c. 1550–1640)

Poets

Edmund Spenser – The Faerie Queene
Sir Philip Sidney – Astrophil and Stella, Defence of Poesy
Walter Raleigh – The Nymph's Reply to the Shepherd
Thomas Sackville – Gorboduc (co-writer)
Michael Drayton – Poly-Olbion

Dramatists

William Shakespeare – All plays and sonnets
Christopher Marlowe – Doctor Faustus, Tamburlaine, The Jew of Malta
Ben Jonson – Volpone, The Alchemist, Bartholomew Fair
John Webster – The Duchess of Malfi, The White Devil
Thomas Kyd – The Spanish Tragedy
George Chapman – Bussy d'Ambois (also Homer translator)
John Lyly – Euphues
Thomas Nashe – The Unfortunate Traveller

Prose Writers & Early Critics

Francis Bacon – Essays, The Advancement of Learning
Richard Hooker – Of the Laws of Ecclesiastical Polity
Stephen Gosson – The School of Abuse (1579)
Sir Philip Sidney – An Apologie for Poetry (1595)
Ben Jonson – Discoveries (Timber)

III. Metaphysical & Cavalier (17th Century)

Metaphysical Poets

John Donne – Songs and Sonnets, Holy Sonnets
George Herbert – The Temple
Andrew Marvell – To His Coy Mistress, Upon Appleton House
Henry Vaughan – Silex Scintillans
Richard Crashaw – Steps to the Temple
Thomas Traherne – Centuries of Meditations

Cavalier Poets

Robert Herrick – Hesperides
Richard Lovelace – To Lucasta
Thomas Carew – Cavalier lyrics
John Suckling – Cavalier lyrics

IV. Puritan & Restoration (c. 1640–1700)

John Milton – Paradise Lost, Paradise Regained, Samson Agonistes
John Bunyan – The Pilgrim's Progress
John Dryden – Absalom and Achitophel, Mac Flecknoe, Essay of Dramatic Poesy
Aphra Behn – Oroonoko, The Rover
Samuel Pepys – Diary
John Evelyn – Diary
Samuel Butler – Hudibras

Restoration Dramatists

William Congreve – The Way of the World
George Etherege – The Man of Mode
William Wycherley – The Country Wife
John Vanbrugh – The Relapse
George Farquhar – The Beaux' Stratagem

V. 18th Century / Augustan & Neoclassical

Novelists

Daniel Defoe – Robinson Crusoe, Moll Flanders
Jonathan Swift – Gulliver's Travels, A Modest Proposal
Samuel Richardson – Pamela, Clarissa
Henry Fielding – Tom Jones, Joseph Andrews
Laurence Sterne – Tristram Shandy
Tobias Smollett – Roderick Random
Fanny Burney – Evelina
Mary Wollstonecraft – A Vindication of the Rights of Woman

Poets

Alexander Pope – The Rape of the Lock, The Dunciad
Thomas Gray – Elegy Written in a Country Churchyard
Oliver Goldsmith – The Deserted Village, She Stoops to Conquer
William Cowper – The Task
Robert Burns – Scottish national poet
William Blake – Songs of Innocence and Experience

Essayists & Critics

Joseph Addison & Richard Steele – The Spectator, The Tatler
Samuel Johnson – Lives of the Poets, A Dictionary, Preface to Shakespeare
Alexander Pope – An Essay on Criticism
Edmund Burke – A Philosophical Enquiry into the Sublime and Beautiful
David Hume – Of the Standard of Taste

VI. Romantic Period (c. 1798–1830)

William Wordsworth – Lyrical Ballads, The Prelude, Preface to Lyrical Ballads
Samuel Taylor Coleridge – The Rime of the Ancient Mariner, Biographia Literaria
Lord Byron – Don Juan, Childe Harold's Pilgrimage
Percy Bysshe Shelley – Ozymandias, Prometheus Unbound
John Keats – Odes (e.g. Ode to a Nightingale)
William Blake – The Marriage of Heaven and Hell
Walter Scott – Waverley, Ivanhoe
Thomas De Quincey – Confessions of an English Opium-Eater
Charles Lamb – Essays of Elia
William Hazlitt – The Spirit of the Age, The Round Table
Leigh Hunt – editor of The Examiner
Immanuel Kant – Critique of Judgement (aesthetic theory)

VII. Victorian Period (c. 1830–1900)

Poets

Alfred Lord Tennyson – In Memoriam, Ulysses
Robert Browning – My Last Duchess, The Ring and the Book
Elizabeth Barrett Browning – Sonnets from the Portuguese
Matthew Arnold – Dover Beach, Culture and Anarchy
Christina Rossetti – Goblin Market
Dante Gabriel Rossetti – Pre-Raphaelite poet
Gerard Manley Hopkins – The Windhover, Pied Beauty
Arthur Hugh Clough – The Bothie of Tober-na-Vuolich
A. C. Swinburne – Poems and Ballads

Novelists

Charles Dickens – Great Expectations, Oliver Twist
Jane Austen – Pride and Prejudice, Emma
Charlotte Brontë – Jane Eyre
Emily Brontë – Wuthering Heights
Anne Brontë – The Tenant of Wildfell Hall
George Eliot – Middlemarch, The Mill on the Floss
Thomas Hardy – Tess of the d'Urbervilles, Jude the Obscure
Mary Shelley – Frankenstein
William Makepeace Thackeray – Vanity Fair
Anthony Trollope – The Warden, Barchester Towers
Wilkie Collins – The Woman in White, The Moonstone
Elizabeth Gaskell – North and South, Mary Barton
George Meredith – The Egoist
Samuel Butler – Erewhon, The Way of All Flesh

Prose Writers, Critics & Essayists

John Ruskin – Modern Painters, The Stones of Venice, Unto This Last
Walter Pater – Studies in the History of the Renaissance
Thomas Carlyle – Sartor Resartus, The French Revolution
John Stuart Mill – On Liberty
Matthew Arnold – Culture and Anarchy, The Function of Criticism
Oscar Wilde – The Picture of Dorian Gray, The Critic as Artist

VIII. Modernist Period (c. 1900–1950)

Poets

W. B. Yeats – The Second Coming, Sailing to Byzantium
T. S. Eliot – The Waste Land, Prufrock, Hamlet and His Problems
Ezra Pound – The Cantos, Hugh Selwyn Mauberley
W. H. Auden – Funeral Blues, The Shield of Achilles
Wilfred Owen – Dulce et Decorum Est
Siegfried Sassoon – war poems
Rupert Brooke – The Soldier
Robert Graves – Goodbye to All That
Dylan Thomas – Do not go gentle into that good night

Novelists

James Joyce – Ulysses, A Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man
Virginia Woolf – Mrs Dalloway, To the Lighthouse, A Room of One's Own
D. H. Lawrence – Sons and Lovers, Lady Chatterley's Lover
Joseph Conrad – Heart of Darkness, Lord Jim
Henry James – The Portrait of a Lady, The Turn of the Screw
E. M. Forster – A Passage to India, Howards End
H. G. Wells – The Time Machine, The War of the Worlds
George Orwell – 1984, Animal Farm
Aldous Huxley – Brave New World
Evelyn Waugh – Brideshead Revisited
Graham Greene – The Heart of the Matter
William Faulkner – The Sound and the Fury
Ernest Hemingway – The Old Man and the Sea
F. Scott Fitzgerald – The Great Gatsby

Dramatists

George Bernard Shaw – Pygmalion, Mrs. Warren's Profession
Oscar Wilde – The Importance of Being Earnest
J. M. Synge – The Playboy of the Western World
Sean O'Casey – Juno and the Paycock
W. B. Yeats – The Countess Cathleen
T. S. Eliot – Murder in the Cathedral

IX. Postmodern & Contemporary (1950–present)

Poets

Ted Hughes – The Hawk in the Rain, Crow
Sylvia Plath – Ariel, The Bell Jar
Allen Ginsberg – Howl
Seamus Heaney – Digging, Death of a Naturalist
Philip Larkin – The Whitsun Weddings
John Betjeman – Collected Poems
Stevie Smith – Not Waving but Drowning
Derek Walcott – Omeros
Margaret Atwood – The Handmaid's Tale (also novelist)
Carol Ann Duffy – The World's Wife
Benjamin Zephaniah – performance poet

Novelists

Ian McEwan – Atonement, Saturday
Somerset Maugham – Of Human Bondage
Kingsley Amis – Lucky Jim
John Fowles – The French Lieutenant's Woman
Doris Lessing – The Golden Notebook
John le Carré – The Spy Who Came in from the Cold
Iris Murdoch – The Sea, The Sea
Salman Rushdie – Midnight's Children
A. S. Byatt – Possession
Kazuo Ishiguro – The Remains of the Day
J. M. Coetzee – Disgrace
Toni Morrison – Beloved, The Bluest Eye
Alice Walker – The Color Purple
Don DeLillo – White Noise
Thomas Pynchon – The Crying of Lot 49
Vladimir Nabokov – Lolita

Dramatists

Samuel Beckett – Waiting for Godot, Endgame
Harold Pinter – The Birthday Party, The Caretaker
John Osborne – Look Back in Anger
Tom Stoppard – Rosencrantz and Guildenstern Are Dead
Caryl Churchill – Top Girls, Cloud Nine
Edward Albee – Who's Afraid of Virginia Woolf?
Arthur Miller – Death of a Salesman, The Crucible
Tennessee Williams – A Streetcar Named Desire
Eugene O'Neill – The Iceman Cometh
David Mamet – Glengarry Glen Ross
Sarah Kane – Blasted

X. American Literature

Early American

Nathaniel Hawthorne – The Scarlet Letter
Herman Melville – Moby-Dick
Edgar Allan Poe – The Raven, short stories
Walt Whitman – Leaves of Grass
Emily Dickinson – complete poems
Henry David Thoreau – Walden
Ralph Waldo Emerson – “Self-Reliance”

Realism & Naturalism

Mark Twain – Adventures of Huckleberry Finn
Theodore Dreiser – Sister Carrie
Stephen Crane – The Red Badge of Courage
Jack London – The Call of the Wild

Modernist & Postmodern

John Steinbeck – The Grapes of Wrath
Wallace Stevens – The Emperor of Ice-Cream
E. E. Cummings – experimental poetry
Robert Frost – The Road Not Taken
Philip Roth – Portnoy's Complaint
Saul Bellow – The Adventures of Augie March
Joyce Carol Oates – Black Water
John Barth – The Sot-Weed Factor
Kurt Vonnegut – Slaughterhouse-Five

XI. Indian Writing in English

Novelists

Mulk Raj Anand – Untouchable, Coolie
R. K. Narayan – The Guide, Swami and Friends
Raja Rao – Kanthapura
Salman Rushdie – Midnight's Children, The Satanic Verses
Arundhati Roy – The God of Small Things
Amitav Ghosh – Sea of Poppies, The Glass Palace
Vikram Seth – A Suitable Boy
Rohinton Mistry – A Fine Balance
Nayantara Sahgal – Rich Like Us
Anita Desai – Clear Light of Day
Shashi Deshpande – The Dark Holds No Terrors
Kamala Markandaya – Nectar in a Sieve
Bhabani Bhattacharya – So Many Hungers!
Manju Kapur – Difficult Daughters
Upamanyu Chatterjee – English, August
Khushwant Singh – Train to Pakistan
G. V. Desani – All About H. Hatterr

Poets

Nissim Ezekiel – The Unfinished Man, Night of the Scorpion
A. K. Ramanujan – The Striders
Dom Moraes – A Beginning
Kamala Das – My Story, The Dance of the Eunuchs
Jayanta Mahapatra – Relationship
Eunice de Souza – poet and critic
Imtiaz Dharker – Purdah
R. Parthasarathy – Rough Passage
Meena Alexander – Illiterate Heart
Keki N. Daruwalla – The Keeper of the Dead
Adil Jussawalla – Land's End
Arun Kolatkar – Jejuri

Dramatists

Girish Karnad – Tughlaq, Hayavadana, Nagamandala
Mahesh Dattani – Final Solutions, Tara
Badal Sircar – Evam Indrajit, Third Theatre
Vijay Tendulkar – Ghashiram Kotwal
Rabindranath Tagore – Chandalika, The Post Office

Prose / Non‑fiction

Rabindranath Tagore – Gitanjali
Nirad C. Chaudhuri – Autobiography of an Unknown Indian
V. S. Naipaul – A House for Mr. Biswas, An Area of Darkness
Pico Iyer – travel writer
Salman Rushdie – essays

XII. Other Postcolonial & Commonwealth

African

Chinua Achebe – Things Fall Apart
Wole Soyinka – A Dance of the Forests, The Lion and the Jewel
Ngũgĩ wa Thiong'o – A Grain of Wheat, Decolonising the Mind
Bessie Head – A Question of Power
Nadine Gordimer – July's People

Caribbean

George Lamming – In the Castle of My Skin
Jean Rhys – Wide Sargasso Sea
Jamaica Kincaid – Annie John

Canadian / Australian / NZ

Alice Munro – Lives of Girls and Women
Michael Ondaatje – The English Patient
Patrick White – Voss, The Tree of Man
Thomas Keneally – Schindler's Ark
A. D. Hope – poet
Katherine Mansfield – short stories

XIII. Literary Critics, Theorists & Essayists

Classical to 19th Century

Plato – The Republic (banishment of poets)
Aristotle – Poetics (mimesis, catharsis)
Horace – Ars Poetica (utile et dulce)
Longinus – On the Sublime
Sir Philip Sidney – An Apologie for Poetry
Stephen Gosson – The School of Abuse
John Dryden – An Essay of Dramatic Poesy
Alexander Pope – An Essay on Criticism
Joseph Addison – “Pleasures of the Imagination”
Samuel Johnson – Lives of the Poets, Preface to Shakespeare
Edmund Burke – On the Sublime and Beautiful
David Hume – Of the Standard of Taste
Immanuel Kant – Critique of Judgement
William Wordsworth – Preface to Lyrical Ballads
S. T. Coleridge – Biographia Literaria (suspension of disbelief)
John Keats – letters on negative capability
Matthew Arnold – Culture and Anarchy, touchstone method
Walter Pater – Studies in the History of the Renaissance
John Ruskin – Modern Painters, The Stones of Venice
Oscar Wilde – The Critic as Artist

Modern Critics (early 20th C.)

T. S. Eliot – Tradition and the Individual Talent (objective correlative)
I. A. Richards – Principles of Literary Criticism, Practical Criticism
William Empson – Seven Types of Ambiguity
F. R. Leavis – The Great Tradition, Scrutiny
Cleanth Brooks – The Well Wrought Urn
W. K. Wimsatt & Monroe Beardsley – “The Intentional Fallacy”, “The Affective Fallacy”
John Crowe Ransom – The New Criticism
Northrop Frye – Anatomy of Criticism (archetypal)
Maud Bodkin – Archetypal Patterns in Poetry
René Wellek & Austin Warren – Theory of Literature

Russian Formalism & Bakhtin

Viktor Shklovsky – “Art as Technique” (defamiliarisation)
Boris Eichenbaum – Theory of the Formal Method
Mikhail Bakhtin – The Dialogic Imagination (dialogism, heteroglossia, carnivalesque, chronotope)

Structuralism & Semiotics

Ferdinand de Saussure – Course in General Linguistics (langue/parole)
Roman Jakobson – linguistics and poetics
Roland Barthes – Mythologies, The Death of the Author, S/Z
Claude Lévi-Strauss – structural anthropology
Tzvetan Todorov – The Typology of Detective Fiction

Post‑Structuralism & Deconstruction

Jacques Derrida – Of Grammatology, “Structure, Sign and Play”
Michel Foucault – The Archaeology of Knowledge, Discipline and Punish
Julia Kristeva – Revolution in Poetic Language (intertextuality, semiotic)
Paul de Man – deconstruction in America
Jean‑François Lyotard – The Postmodern Condition

Psychoanalytic Criticism

Sigmund Freud – id, ego, superego, Oedipus complex
Jacques Lacan – mirror stage, the symbolic order

Marxist Criticism

Karl Marx – The Communist Manifesto, Das Kapital
Georg Lukács – The Theory of the Novel (reflection realism)
Louis Althusser – Ideological State Apparatuses, interpellation
Antonio Gramsci – cultural hegemony
Theodor W. Adorno – Aesthetic Theory, culture industry
Walter Benjamin – “The Work of Art in the Age of Mechanical Reproduction” (aura)
Fredric Jameson – The Political Unconscious, Postmodernism
Raymond Williams – Culture and Society, structure of feeling

Feminist & Gender Theory

Mary Wollstonecraft – A Vindication of the Rights of Woman
Virginia Woolf – A Room of One's Own (Shakespeare’s sister)
Simone de Beauvoir – The Second Sex
Hélène Cixous – “The Laugh of the Medusa” (écriture feminine)
Luce Irigaray – Speculum of the Other Woman
Elaine Showalter – A Literature of Their Own, gynocriticism
Sandra Gilbert & Susan Gubar – The Madwoman in the Attic
Judith Butler – Gender Trouble (gender performativity)

Postcolonial Theory

Frédéric Fanon – Black Skin, White Masks, The Wretched of the Earth
Aimé Césaire – Discourse on Colonialism (Négritude)
Edward Said – Orientalism
Gayatri Chakravorty Spivak – Can the Subaltern Speak?
Homi K. Bhabha – The Location of Culture (hybridity, third space)
Ngũgĩ wa Thiong'o – Decolonising the Mind

Reader‑Response, Cultural Studies & Others

Wolfgang Iser – The Act of Reading
Stanley Fish – Is There a Text in This Class?
Richard Hoggart – The Uses of Literacy
Stuart Hall – encoding/decoding, cultural identity
Terry Eagleton – Literary Theory: An Introduction
Jonathan Culler – Structuralist Poetics, Literary Theory: A Very Short Introduction
Bruno Latour – Actor‑Network Theory (ANT)

Indian Critics & Theoreticians

M. K. Naik – A History of Indian English Literature
G. N. Devy – After Amnesia
Meenakshi Mukherjee – The Twice Born Fiction
K. R. Srinivasa Iyengar – Indian Writing in English
Gauri Viswanathan – Masks of Conquest
Braj Kachru – World Englishes model
Sharankumar Limbale – Towards an Aesthetic of Dalit Literature
Sisir Kumar Das – A History of Indian Literature (volumes)
Harish Trivedi – Colonial Transactions
Makarand Paranjape – Towards a Poetics of the Indian English Novel

XIV. Periodicals, Journalists & Essayists

Joseph Addison & Richard Steele – The Spectator, The Tatler
Samuel Johnson – The Rambler, The Idler
James Boswell – Life of Johnson
Charles Lamb – Essays of Elia
William Hazlitt – The Plain Speaker, The Round Table
Leigh Hunt – The Examiner
Thomas Carlyle – essays
John Stuart Mill – The Westminster Review
George Orwell – essays and journalism
Virginia Woolf – essays
T. S. Eliot – The Criterion
F. R. Leavis – Scrutiny

I have prompted DeepSeek generative AI to reference the PYQs and this list to form short notes on these topics, it's doing a great job in gathering the info but not very much on getting the big picture. I'll update the list and make it comprehensive.

Oh! this newness burdens my brain, it feels like a rock on my head, I can feel the overwhelming weight on my head. Take a break, make it lighter and do the familiar.

I still feel dumb, is it the burn out phase or I just want something else to do. It's like the mental fog that blurs my mind.

Previous night I gave a test and got 90 out of 150. The spirit of urgency died. Now I feel calmer than ever before. I don't know what happened. I can't fix my head on any topic. This means its time to take some new field approach. My work of NET is over now.
